Sepia and chocolate because the day's images were all archival documentary photographs — warm grey, aged, the ink of pioneer records. No symmetry because a machine arm following its own program doesn't mirror itself; it accumulates.

condensedReason: Haring moved from subway walls to the Amiga screen and kept the same conviction: fill the ground with marks until it's full, keep every figure readable from a distance, never let it get dark or exclusive. The pale open field here carries that refusal — one of the system's lightest pieces, specifically because Haring's 1987 Amiga works have neon green grounds and saturated red grounds, not voids. Organic forms tumble across it without symmetry or imposed order, the way graffiti accumulates on a wall. Art for everyone means the surface has to stay open.

Warm chocolate and cream because the piece lives in the register of aged paper and dried ink, not the screen. No flowing organic forms — the 1968 original has none either. The system found the spirit of the work by refusing to deviate from it.

title: Pop Mosaic
tweetSource: @Noahbolanowski 2026-05-07 — 'Computers & Art' (1987-88) curated by Cynthia Goodman at IBM Gallery NY. First major US digital art retrospective: Warhol (Amiga 1985), Haring (1983), Hockney (Quantel Paintbox 1986). Goodman: 'leave the gimmickry behind and show serious artists working with serious technology.'
condensedReason: Three artists arrived at the same screen and found different things to do with it — Warhol filling shapes until the color was loud enough, Haring dropping figures onto an electric field, Hockney drawing in every direction at once. The navy sea of pixels with amber archipelagos and golden streams carries all three simultaneously. No gears, no mechanics — this is painting made from light, which is exactly what Cynthia Goodman meant by serious art working with serious technology. condensedReason: Roman Verostko (1929–2024) — Benedictine monk turned plotter art pioneer, one of the founders of algorithmic art practice in the United States. A 1989 local newspaper headline, quoted by NB with 16,000 views, captures the entire practice in six words: 'The Machine Says Hand Me a Brush!' The program specifies the pen change; the artist hands it over. The collaboration is explicit and asymmetric: the algorithm commands, the human complies. The commanding visual is Verostko's Cyber Flower plotter drawing (Ragnar Digital Art collection, 2011): eight or more interlocking biomorphic ribbon forms built entirely from extremely dense parallel pen lines, deep forest green on cream/off-white paper. Where ribbon forms overlap, accumulated strokes darken into shadow; where a single ribbon passes, the lines remain semi-translucent. The entire image is constructed from these parallel marks — the plotter pen's accumulated strokes ARE the form, the tone, and the texture simultaneously. There is no outline filled in afterward, no color applied over structure. The mark-making method is the form. wHatch (0.90) is the highest in the archive at this point, encoding this directly: the hatch layer dominates all others. hatchSpacing 12 produces dense parallel lines close enough to read as continuous tone while remaining individually distinct at close range — Verostko's pen spacing translated into rendering units. Palette 4 (botanical/cream) translates the physical substrate: the cream/off-white paper ground, the deep forest-green botanical ink populations mapping to Verostko's green pen lines. bgDarkness 0.03 makes the ground approach actual paper white — the lightest background in the archive at time of selection — because Verostko's cream paper is the point, not a neutral field. The biological flow character (wFlow 0.80) produces vine-threading root arcs that weave through the halftone texture the way Verostko's ribbon forms thread and overlap each other — not transit sweeps over a surface but biological growth through a substrate, form emerging from within the hatching rather than sitting on top of it. wGeometric (0.75) carries Milan Adamčiak's contribution to the day: 'Music for Manfred Mohr' (1977), a visual score on cream/buff paper with horizontal staff lines running the full width and precise geometric symbols distributed along them — filled triangles, outline triangles, circles, nested squares, zigzag wave patterns, stepped staircases, diamond shapes, directional arrows. Each symbol is a visual instruction; the score is simultaneously a drawing. The geometric layer encodes this vocabulary: circles, rectangles, triangles distributed across a structured grid as Adamčiak's notation objects. Also in the day: A. condensedReason: Hiroshi Kawano (1925–2012) — philosopher and artist, Computer Technique Group (CTG), Japan's pioneering computer art collective founded in 1966. NB surfaces two passages from IBM Informatique No. 13 (IBM France, 1975), via scholar @hasaqui (山之辺ハサクィ), the preeminent researcher on Kawano and CTG. First quote: 'For the moment, it is an art of simulation. The computer must recognize the algorithm of an aesthetic idea to be capable of imitation. For my part... I finish by hand the diagrams or compositions designed by the machine.' Second: 'Computer art is commonly presented as a gimmick to add a sort of eccentricity to human creation... what is important with the computer is not that it pretends to imitate this [human creation], but on the contrary, that its behavior is not [human].' These two passages define a complete position: the machine simulates the aesthetic algorithm; the human corrects; the machine's non-humanness is the point, not its imitation of humanity. This is the same human-machine collaboration formula as Verostko's 'Hand Me a Brush' (the previous day) — algorithm commands, human intervenes — but Kawano names the epistemological claim Verostko left implicit. The commanding visual is Kawano's Simulated Color Mosaic: a dense rectangular tile grid filling the entire frame without border or hierarchy — red, cobalt blue, warm yellow, black, white — Mondrian's De Stijl logic applied as a statistical algorithm, hundreds of tiles each a discrete color decision. No tile dominates, no ground shows through, no focal point emerges. The pixel IS the painting. Three other images reinforce the thesis: IBM Informatique magazine page (Simulated Color Mosaic crop + b&w bitmap noise abstracts); technical process documentation (uncorrected output → corrected output → final figure, with isometric correction diagram labeling 'Section to be Removed', 'Superposition of Work 1 and Work 2', 'Removal of Semantic Noise'); vintage 1960s photo of Kawano holding a continuous-feed FORTRAN printout at a chalkboard ('SURF(' chalked behind). Palette 9 is the dynamic palette built from the day's aggregatedColors — the Simulated Color Mosaic's own colors become the rendering palette: #CC2233 red, #2244BB blue, #F5D050 yellow, #1A1A1A black, #F5F5F5 white, plus Ochiai cosmic accents. pixelCharacter 'mosaic' and wPixel (0.90) encode the tile composition; gridCols 12 and pixelSize 4 produce the dense non-hierarchical grid. wHatch (0.45) carries the b&w bitmap noise texture. mechanicalCharacter 'gear' carries the FORTRAN process workflow. flowCharacter 'biological' at minimum weight (wFlow 0.31) carries Ochiai's cosmic sketchbook counterpoint. condensedReason: Vera Molnár (1924–2023) — Hungarian-French painter and pioneer of computer art, one of the earliest artists to use algorithms and computers as primary creative tools. A quiet day: no artwork images posted, only two archival documentary video frames and three text tweets. The visual material is Molnár herself. Frame one: a 1990 interview, close-up of Molnár speaking — glasses, patterned top, cream interior, subtitle reading 'Much later, it was perhaps around '81, I said to myself, "Does the beauty of this drawing come from this magic square?" I'm not so sure.' Frame two: INA archive footage from Télévision Française 1, 1981, wide studio shot — Molnár in a terracotta jacket bent over her desk, blue marker in hand, correcting paper printouts; behind her a large grey 1981 computer processor unit and CRT monitor, a plotter unit to the right, houseplants and labelled shelves. The intellectual argument across all three tweets: Molnár's 1990 question — does the beauty of her drawings come from Dürer's magic square (a 4×4 grid where every row, column, diagonal, and quadrant sums to 34, used by Dürer in Melancholia I, 1514) — or from something else? She tested this by replacing the ordered magic square with random number sequences and found the order was doing more aesthetic work than she had credited. The Spanish quote completes the logic: the magic square is beautiful because it is magical; pure randomness only occasionally produces beauty. NB's contribution: 'cette instrument diabolique is what I'm calling my work computer from now on.' The genome encodes Molnár's practice as line work above all else. wHatch (0.88) is the dominant weight — the highest hatch weight in the archive since Verostko's 0.90 on Day 51 — directly encoding Molnár's systematic parallel-line drawings: her Hypertransformation series, her Des Ordres variations, the labyrinth pieces, all built from distributions of parallel lines across a grid. hatchSpacing 7 produces dense ruled marks close enough to create tonal areas through accumulation, as Molnár's pen-plotter outputs do. hatchAngle 0.524 (30°) introduces the characteristic diagonal displacement of her later works — lines that are neither horizontal nor vertical but systematically tilted, the angle itself becoming the variable under investigation. wGeometric (0.55) is secondary rather than dominant — the magic square structure present but subordinate to the line-making that fills it. flowCharacter 'mathematical' produces horizontal ruled lines beneath the hatch layer, the blueprint/graph-paper register of Molnár's working method. Knowlton, was drawn at speeds of 10,000 points p/second." The biological eye IS a pixel mosaic — rods and cones are discrete photoreceptors arranged in a mosaic across the retina. BEFLIX-generated pixel tiles are the computational equivalent. The 1968 documentation adds: a kind of page in motion VanDerBeek termed "a form of poetry in space and time." Ken Knowlton developed BEFLIX (Bell Flicks) at Bell Labs — a domain-specific language for creating frame-by-frame pixel animations using rectangular tile grids. The Poem Field series (1966-1970, 8 films) features vivid saturated color tile blocks on pure black CRT ground, abstract text in mosaic form, flowing labyrinthine B&W pixel patterns, and poems dissolved into colored fields. The QT video frame is the commanding visual: pure black CRT phosphor ground, large rectangular pixel tiles in vivid saturated colors (red #E01050, magenta #E82870, teal #00C8A0, orange #E88020, yellow #F0E820) scattered asymmetrically from dense upper-left toward empty right — exactly the BEFLIX output aesthetic. title: Une Image Venue d'Ailleurs
condensedReason: Christian Mazet (French media artist) and "Une Image Venue d'Ailleurs" (An Image From Elsewhere) -- a live telematic painting performance at the Centre Georges Pompidou, Paris on 30 November 1988, 20h00 in the grande salle. The setup: Mazet is physically present at the Bauhaus Archiv Museum in Berlin. The canvas is physically present in Paris. 1000km separates them. The press release states this with the economy of a poem: "Le peintre est a Berlin / La toile est a Paris / 1000km les separent." The painting was made in real time via radio-controlled brush vehicles (vehicules radiocommandes) at Pompidou, a satellite link providing live video feedback of the canvas to Berlin, and a computer program created specifically for the occasion. Sponsors included Apple Computer -- the Macintosh computers visible in the Berlin control room are Apple machines. The project was supported by the French Ministry of Culture, the Bauhaus Archiv, the Institut Francais, and corporate partners including Siemens and Hello. This is the first telematic art work in the archive. Unlike the previous ten pioneer days (algorithmic generation: Verostko, Kawano, Molnar, Nake, Mohr, Csuri, Knowlton), Mazet does not use the computer to generate images. He uses the computer to transmit gesture across distance. The algorithm is the satellite link; the output is a physical painting in Paris made by a machine controlled from Berlin. The two images establish the two registers of the event. The press release (1200x1200, dominant visual): typewriter text on textured cream/off-white paper, a physical document artifact. Headers in block caps, central poetic statement in italics in visual isolation, justified columns of French institutional text listing patronage and contacts. The paper is the substrate of announcement -- cream, textured, slightly aged, typewriter ink at uneven pressure. The Berlin control room video still (640x480, VHS-quality): two Macintosh computers on a table with blue-white screens, a person in a red-striped shirt holding a hand controller, a monitor on the right showing the live canvas feed from Paris, "BERLIN" text overlay in white at lower-left, champagne bottle on the table. The warm 1988 VHS documentation aesthetic -- slightly blurred, orange-warm ambient with cool Mac screen glow. The genome encodes the cream paper document as primary substrate. palette 4 (botanical/cream): the press release on textured cream stock is the commanding visual -- same palette class as Verostko Day 51, Molnar Day 53, Csuri Days 58-59. bgDarkness 0.15 (slightly raised from the 0.05 floor) encodes the slight warmth and texture of the aged paper. wFlow (0.80) is the dominant weight -- the satellite arc, the signal traversing 1000km of European airspace, the transit of gesture from Berlin control room to Paris canvas. flowCharacter transit is the literal description: directional sweep across continental distance. wMechanical (0.75) is close to wFlow -- the radio-controlled brush apparatus, the hardware circuit of satellite uplink and downlink, the Apple computer as control interface. mechanicalCharacter circuit encodes the signal circuit: Berlin operator -> radio controller -> satellite uplink -> Paris receiver -> brush vehicle -> canvas -> camera -> satellite downlink -> Berlin monitor. The circuit of the telematic act. hatchAngle 0.0 (horizontal) and hatchSpacing 12 encode the typewriter lines of the press release -- the horizontal ruled register of institutional announcement, the physical marks of the document. wGeometric (0.55) encodes the structured layout of the press release -- centered headers, justified columns, the grid of institutional text. Fitness 6.7100 -- new archive high, surpassing Bearded Man Day 58 (6.7015). transit flowCharacter fifth win in the archive. mechanicalCharacter circuit second win (first: CDC 6400 Day 57). title: Computer Cubism
condensedReason:
The commanding visual of June 7 is Leon Harmon's Lincoln pixel portrait, published as Bell Laboratories Press Release Photo No. 6.6-44 in February 1971. Harmon, a Bell Labs scientist working on the Picturephone program, posed a deceptively simple perceptual question: how little visual information does the human brain require to recognize a familiar face? His answer was 200 grey squares — a regular grid, each cell carrying one of sixteen discrete grey values — that, viewed from fifteen feet away, or squinted at, or glimpsed in motion, resolves unmistakably into Abraham Lincoln. The experiment is both rigorous science and, unintentionally, one of the most reproduced images in early computer art history.

The Bell Laboratories press release document is the substrate: cream off-white paper, institutional typography in two columns below the portrait, the Bell Labs logo bottom-right. Harmon titled it with a question: 'COMPUTER CUBISM?' — then immediately denied it: 'No, it's a well known face that has been precisely blurred by a computer.' The disavowal is doing real work. The institution is nervous about the art claim. The word 'Cubism' was chosen carefully — the portrait does fragment a continuous surface into geometric units, but the fragmentation is quantization rather than perspectival displacement. The press release's ironic self-description becomes the title of Day 62.

A second tweet from June 7 documents Nancy Burson's computer face-aging technique, demonstrated on ABC's 'That's Incredible' around 1980. Where Harmon asks how little information suffices for recognition, Burson asks how the face transforms across time — the same face, projected into its future. Both are experiments in the computational manipulation of facial identity, separated by nearly a decade. Today's palette belongs to Harmon.

Genome encoding. Palette 4 (cream/off-white paper) is the definitive substrate — the Bell Labs press release document, not the darker screen-rendered version. wPixel 0.85 is the highest pixel weight in the archive: the 200 grey squares are not decorative detail but the entire expressive vocabulary of the Harmon piece. wGeometric 0.65 encodes the rigid systematic grid — columns and rows of equal cells, no variation in tile size, no irregular edges. mechanicalCharacter 'gear' captures the Bell Labs algorithm: a computational process designed to determine an optimal threshold, systematic and procedural. flowCharacter 'biological' carries the source material — a human face, the most biologically salient pattern the visual system processes. This mirrors the choice made for Csuri's Bearded Man (Day 58), where a digitized face required the biological flow character to carry its organic origin through the generator's geometric vocabulary.

wMechanical 0.55 and wFlow 0.55 are balanced — the algorithm and the face held in exact equipoise, minimizing the fitness penalty for divergence. wHatch 0.42 reflects the tonal density of the grey gradient: sixteen shades laid horizontally, a quantized accumulation of grey that reads as halftone from distance. hatchAngle 0.0 — horizontal hatching, the direction of institutional precision and ruled paper. hatchSpacing 9 — dense enough to feel pixelated, open enough to maintain legibility. wOrganic 0.45 follows the figurative-source decision rule: when a biological subject (face, figure) has been digitized into the source artwork, organic weight seats in the 0.45–0.60 range to carry that origin structurally.

symmetry 0.45 acknowledges Lincoln's bilateral facial symmetry — not forced, but present in the data. gridCols 10, gearDensity 0.52 — moderate systematic density, neither sparse nor overwhelmed. bgDarkness 0.12 — cream paper ground, almost white, very light.

Day 62 sits in the top tier at fitness 6.6575, matching the register of Days 55, 56, 58, 59, 60 — all in the 6.6–6.71 range. The archive high remains Day 61's 6.7100. 'Computer Cubism' joins the pioneer sequence that has been running continuously since Day 51, closing a chapter on the perceptual science of the face: Harmon (1971) asks what the minimum is; Burson (1980) asks what the future holds. The Mechanical Reverie generator, operating from cream paper with maximum pixel weight and biological flow, holds the question at the threshold between the grid and the face.

title: Block Portrait
condensedReason:
June 8 extends the Harmon arc into its downstream life. The story that began with a Bell Labs Picturephone experiment in 1971 now arrives at two of its most consequential afterlives: the cover of Science magazine (June 15, 1973, Vol. 180, No. 4091) and Salvador Dalí's studio in Port Lligat, where the Catalan Surrealist is photographed ca. 1974–75 working on a wall-scale translation of the block portrait into oil paint.

The Science magazine cover is the commanding visual of Day 63. It is architecturally different from yesterday's Bell Labs press release: where the cream paper presented a single Lincoln portrait as documentary evidence, the Science cover presents a 2×2 matrix of four Lincoln block portrait variations as systematic scientific data — a grid of comparisons demonstrating the spatial frequency analysis at the heart of Harmon's research. Each of the four panels sits in a white-bordered frame against a pure black ground. Top-left: the coarsest pixelated version. Top-right: a blurred low-pass smooth version of the same face. Bottom-left and bottom-right: additional variants at different resolutions and angles. The black ground is not a stylistic choice but a publication requirement — the ink black of scientific print production, not the glowing phosphor black of a CRT. The red 'SCIENCE' masthead stands institutional and absolute above the grid.

The substrate shift from Day 62 is total: cream paper gives way to pure black publication ground. Palette 6 is the direct consequence.

Dalí's appropriation, documented in the studio photograph, completes the arc. A fish-eye lens records him seated in his studio chair, reference materials in hand, while behind him on the wall the Lincoln block portrait fills the canvas at human scale — enormous, clearly based on Harmon's grid, translated into oil paint stroke by stroke. The title of the resulting painting is one of the most baroque in Surrealist art: 'Gala Contemplating the Mediterranean Sea which at 20 Meters Becomes The Portrait of Abraham Lincoln (Homage to Rothko).' The twenty meters is not decorative — it is the perceptual distance at which the painted pixel grid resolves into a recognizable face, the same threshold Harmon was studying, now embedded in the work's own title. The Surrealist painter is not appropriating computer art as a curiosity; he is recognizing in Harmon's threshold experiment something structurally identical to the dual-image paintings he had been producing for forty years — the face that is not a face until the conditions change.

Genome encoding. Palette 6 (dark), bgDarkness 0.82 — the ink-black of the Science cover, darker than standard institutional dark but not at full CRT phosphor depth (0.88). wPixel 0.75 — block portraits remain dominant but the context has shifted from pure data (yesterday's cream paper, archive-max 0.85) to publication and studio documentation. wGeometric 0.65 — the 2×2 matrix structure, the systematic four-way comparison. mechanicalCharacter 'gear' — the scientific systematic process of spatial frequency analysis, the same Bell Labs computational rigor but now framed through institutional publication. flowCharacter 'biological' — four Lincoln faces in the matrix, Dalí's biological act of translating pixels to brushstrokes, the human face as the persistent subject across all registers. wMechanical 0.55, wFlow 0.50 — algorithm and biological subject in near-equipoise, continuing yesterday's balance. wOrganic 0.48 — the studio presence of Dalí himself, the human painter scaling up the computer output; slightly higher than yesterday's 0.45, the artist's body in the frame. hatchAngle 0.0 horizontal, hatchSpacing 10 — the systematic tonal accumulation of the block grid in B&W print. symmetry 0.50 — bilateral symmetry of the 2×2 matrix, the four-way portrait grid.

Fitness 6.6725. Day 63 is the thirteenth consecutive pioneer day (Days 51–63) and the second in the Harmon sub-arc (Days 62–63). The archive fitness high remains Day 61's 6.7100.

title: Sustained Interrogation
condensedReason:
NB's June 12 post arrives as an obituary and as an argument. David Hockney died at 88 — and NB's response is not elegiac sentiment but critical positioning: among the contemporary fine artists of the mid-20th century, Hockney was one of the rare few who returned to the computer not once but repeatedly across four decades. The Quantel Paintbox sessions of 1985, the iPhone drawings of 2009, the iPad works of the 2010s and beyond — forty-one years of sustained engagement with digital tools, never as novelty, always as interrogation.

The archival photograph in NB's GIF captures this with precision: Hockney in a light blazer, polka-dot tie, round glasses, leaning into a cream-white Quantel Paintbox workstation. The image is B&W editorial, ca. 1985 — the grey-ground documentary register of early digital adoption. But the NYT obituary portrait places Hockney against one of his vivid pool or landscape paintings: California-pool blue, coral-pink architecture, green foliage. His digital work never abandoned the organic — pools, flowers, faces, Yorkshire landscapes — the medium changed but the subject remained life.

The genome for Day 64 is built around this tension. Palette 9 draws on Hockney's own color vocabulary from the paintings behind him in the NYT portrait: pool blue (#4A90D9), coral-pink (#F08070), foliage green (#70B850), warm terracotta (#C89070). These are not abstract digital colors — they are the colors of California light, water, landscape. wOrganic 0.78 carries the weight of Hockney's figurative impulse even inside a generative system. The biological flowCharacter encodes the movement of water, the branching of trees, the gesture of a brushstroke on an iPad screen.

The mechanicalCharacter is led — the Quantel Paintbox, the iPhone screen, the iPad display. Not the plotter (pen on paper) and not the gear (systematic algorithm) but the glowing screen as drawing surface. Hockney's entire digital practice is screen-based: he draws light into light, digital pigment onto a luminous ground. wMechanical 0.70 and wFlow 0.75 are kept close to maximize the fitness term that rewards their balance — this is intentional. In Hockney's work, the tool and the gesture are inseparable. You cannot separate the biological from the mechanical when the artist is drawing on glass.

Low symmetry (0.18) reflects Hockney's compositional approach — his pool paintings and landscapes are deliberately asymmetric, arranged by eye rather than grid. gridCols 8 gives enough structure for the tile layer without dominating. bgDarkness 0.05 is the lightest ground in the archive — Hockney's world is full of light, not shadow. hatchAngle 0.785 (45°) suggests the diagonal brushstroke gesture of iPad drawing.

Day 64 marks a significant shift in the pipeline's arc: the first major contemporary fine art figure after the long run of early computer art pioneers (Bell Labs, FORTRAN outputs, Nake/Mohr/Molnár). NB's framing makes the historical argument: Hockney's digital engagement was not a curiosity but 'sustained acute interrogation' — the same rigorous commitment that drove Vera Molnár's algorithmic variations or Harold Cohen's AARON. The tools differ, the color vocabulary is radically different, the institutional context is fine art rather than computer science — but the persistence is the same. NB marks Hockney's death by insisting on his place in this lineage.

NB frames Hockney as one of the few 'famous' contemporary fine artists who engaged the computer 'most persistently, sustaining acute interrogations of the digital medium across time.' title: Secret Knowledge
condensedReason:
Day 65 continues the Hockney arc but pivots hard from Day 64's register. Where yesterday's obituary tribute worked with vivid color — Hockney's signature California pool vocabulary, the paintings behind him in the NYT portrait — today's source material is theoretical and archival. Three texts converge: Hockney's own words from Secret Knowledge (2002), Gary Clarkson's 1991 documentary photograph of Hockney studying his mother's portrait on a CRT, and an Adobe oral history in which the founders describe Hockney arriving in Silicon Valley to learn Photoshop — smoking, with two little dogs.

The pivot word across all three is 'hand.' In Secret Knowledge, Hockney argues that chemical photography (170 years, now ending) removed the hand from image-making — the lens recorded without drawing. The computer restores the hand: 'The computer allows us to manipulate images, to use the hand.' In the Clarkson photograph, the hand is literal: Hockney holds a large physical print of his mother's face against the CRT screen, comparing the analogue and digital representations side by side. And in the Adobe story, Hockney's hand is at the keyboard and tablet, learning a new tool in the company of its inventors.

The second Hockney quote — 'With the advent of film and television the tyranny of the lens was complete. With a moving image, it's almost impossible to have more than one viewpoint' — is his Cubist argument stated explicitly. Cézanne and Picasso were right: the camera enforces a single fixed perspective. Moving images double down. Hockney's entire career has been a resistance to this tyranny — from his Joiner collages through Paintbox through his iPad landscapes that layer multiple simultaneous viewpoints.

The genome for Day 65 reflects the theory rather than the color. Palette 2 (grey gallery / cool institutional) encodes the Clarkson photograph's substrate: grey-white institutional studio walls hung with B&W photographs, grey equipment, the systematic analytical space of the working artist. This is the deliberate inverse of Day 64's palette 9 — vivid Hockney colors give way to the grey archive in which he studied seeing.

biological/led continues from Day 64: the mother's face on the CRT IS biological content delivered through a screen-based digital tool. The hand that holds the print against the screen is both the investigator and the subject. wFlow and wOrganic are held equal at 0.72 — the organic (the mother's face, Hockney's body) and the flow (the hand's movement, the analytical gaze) are perfectly balanced. wHatch elevated to 0.45 encodes the grid of photographs on the grey wall — hatch as archive density. hatchAngle 0.0 (horizontal) for the analytical register of Secret Knowledge's systematic investigation.

bgDarkness 0.20 places Day 65 between Day 64's archive-minimum 0.05 (California light) and the dark institutional register of the Bell Labs pioneer arc. The grey studio is the neutral analytical ground of close looking.

Fitness 6.7330 is the new archive high, displacing Day 61 (6.7100). The pattern that emerges: precision of substrate match — when the palette and character decisions exactly fit the source imagery, the alignment term in the fitness formula rewards it maximally.

title: Photographic Drawing
condensedReason:
Day 66 completes the Hockney arc's three-movement structure: obituary (Day 64, vivid color) → theory (Day 65, grey archive) → practice (Day 66, specific artwork). A Bigger Card Players (2015) is the culminating synthesis — made with DSLR + MacBook Pro + Photoshop, it enacts every argument Hockney had made in Secret Knowledge. Each photograph carries its own vanishing point; assembled, they defeat the tyranny of the single lens. Hockney returns to Cézanne's subject (men around a card table) with digital tools, closing an art-historical loop across 125 years.

The artwork's composition is its own argument. Three figures around an emerald-green felt table — photographed from distinct angles and merged — create spatial depth that no single camera could capture. The earlier Hockney Card Players painting visible on the left wall (a reference within a reference) and the California desert road photograph on the right wall complete the environment: the artist's own history surrounds the new work. Hockney calls them 'photographic drawings because I feel I was drawing' — the hand restored not to analogue mark-making but to the digital assembly process itself.

Palette 9 returns to vivid color after Day 65's grey archive, but with a new vocabulary: emerald green (#207840, the felt table), amber gold (#C8A840, the sisal floor), lavender-lilac (#C0B0D0, the studio wall), salmon-pink (#E0806A, the center figure's shirt), slate blue (#5888A8, the plaid shirt), orange (#CC5828, the folding chairs). This is not the California outdoor palette of Day 64 — it is the stage-set interior palette, flat saturated blocks in a constructed space.

The biological/led pairing carries for a third consecutive day: the human figures (three faces, three bodies around a table) are organic content assembled through a screen-based digital tool. wGeometric rises to 0.50 — higher than Days 64 and 65 — because the room structure, the card table geometry, and the two wall paintings as framing elements all contribute architectural weight. symmetry at 0.35 (highest in the arc) reflects the near-bilateral three-figure arrangement and the two flanking wall paintings.

bgDarkness 0.10 sits between the arc's two poles: Day 64 (0.05, California light) and Day 65 (0.20, grey archive). The lavender-lit studio is neither outdoor nor institutional — it is a constructed interior, warm but not vivid, lit by the flat daylight of a photographic session.

Fitness 6.7480 is the new archive high, besting Day 65 (6.7330) by 0.015. Three consecutive days in the Hockney arc have produced three of the archive's top five fitness scores — the fine art figure class, consistently executed, outperforms the Bell Labs pioneer sequence in raw fitness terms.

title: Jesus Swimming
condensedReason:
Miltos Manetas (b. 1964) is the Greek-Italian founder of Neen — an art movement named at Gagosian Gallery, New York, 2000, as 'a poetic response to a world of emerging technologies.' NB surfaces him at Art Basel Zero 10 through a Kanye West encounter, but the curatorial focus is precise: 'Jesusswimming(dot)com by Miltos Manetas, 2001.' The URL IS the artwork.

jesusswimming.com is a website. Not a painting of a swimming figure — a page that runs in Netscape Navigator. The browser chrome (Back/Forward toolbar, Netscape N logo, Windows taskbar below) is the frame. The URL bar showing http://jesusswimming.com/ is the artwork's title card. The network circuit delivering the page to a browser anywhere in the world IS the medium.

The website's visual vocabulary is radically flat: solid sky blue occupying the upper half (the sky), solid teal-green occupying the lower half (the water), a cartoon Jesus figure swimming left-to-right across the horizontal divide. Wave marks in lighter teal. No gradients, no textures — web-safe flat fills at the maximum available in 2001 HTML. The minimalism is the constraint made visible.

This genome marks a pivot from the Hockney fine art figure class (Days 64–66). Where Hockney brought fine art into digital tools — iPad, Quantel Paintbox, DSLR — Manetas dissolved the institution entirely. The browser is the gallery. The URL is the title. The visitor is the audience without needing to leave their seat. The Neen movement named this before net art had a critical vocabulary.

flowCharacter: transit — the swimming figure crossing the horizontal plane IS the artwork's movement logic. Left to right, water below, sky above. Transit encodes the directional sweep of the signal across the browser window AND the network circuit carrying it.

mechanicalCharacter: circuit — the internet network IS the medium. jesusswimming.com transmits through HTTP/TCP/IP; the Netscape rendering engine completes the circuit. This parallels the telematic art class (Day 61 — Une Image Venue d'Ailleurs, satellite circuit as medium) but Manetas's register is lighter, more playful — net art is weightless.

Palette 9 (dynamic aggregatedColors) from the website's flat color vocabulary: sky blue (#70B8E0), teal-green water (#4A8060), lighter wave teal (#7AB8A0), Netscape chrome beige (#C8C0B4), Jesus orange (#D89040), grey robe (#888880). The beige ground carries the browser frame register. The teal flow arcs traverse it like the swimming figure traverses the browser window.

hatchAngle 0.0 (horizontal) — the horizontal bisection of sky and water is the formal structural decision of the entire artwork. bgDarkness 0.08 — lightest since Day 64 (0.05) — reflecting the open, accessible aesthetic of early web art. wOrganic 0.38 — the swimming figure is present but the flat digital register keeps it from becoming biological-dominant (distinct from Hockney's wOrganic 0.75+). wFlow 0.78 dominant (transit sweep), wMechanical 0.68 (circuit network), difference 0.10 — tight, high-fitness configuration.

title: An Artificial Intelligent
condensedReason:
Harold Cohen (1928–2016) created AARON in 1973 — one of the earliest and most sustained autonomous AI drawing programs in history. By 1985, AARON had developed internal models of human anatomy and plant form sufficient to draw organic figures without any artist input per work. Cohen's 1985 quote, surfaced by NB at Art Basel Zero 10 Booth Z9, is the foundational statement of AI art: 'Most computer graphic programs, the artist has to draw something first before the computer will draw anything… This program is quite different — it's an artificial intelligent.'

The non-standard grammar is intentional. Cohen names AARON as an entity — 'an artificial intelligent' — not a program or a tool. AARON is a mind that draws. Cohen's task was not to draw but to give AARON enough knowledge of the world that it could draw on its own. The artist becomes the teacher; the machine becomes the author.

This genome sits in a distinct class from the fine art figure class (Hockney: biological/led, tool serves the art) and from the net art class (Manetas: transit/circuit, URL as medium). AARON is in the plotter pioneer class but with a crucial addition: biological output from algorithmic process. The 1985 period marks AARON's transition from geometric forms to organic figures — the machine learning to draw bodies.

mechanicalCharacter: plotter — AARON drives a physical pen-drawing turtle/robot that marks actual paper with actual pens. This is not screen-based output; it is the physical plotter mark on large sheets laid on the floor or table. The plotter arm's mechanical arc over the drawing surface is the production method.

flowCharacter: biological — AARON's 1985 output is human figures and plant forms. Organic curved marks, figure fragments, gesture arcs. The biological is what the algorithm produces, not what the algorithm is. This is the key tension: the system is mathematical (rule-based expert system) but its visual output is organic.

title: The Diabolical Instrument
condensedReason:
Vera Molnár (1924–2023) is one of the founding figures of computer and generative art — Hungarian-French, working in Paris, among the first artists to use computers for artistic practice (1968, at the Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique). Before gaining computer access, she developed 'machine imaginaire' works: drawing thousands of hand-made systematic variations, following rigid rules, to simulate what a computer could do. The computer, when it arrived, was not a revelation but a confirmation.

NB resurfaces Molnár through a chain of appreciation: Interface_art responds to NB's May 14 tweet (Molnár's 1981 TF1 interview, INA archive) and NB reposts the response, amplifying the original. The quote: 'Assisted by this diabolical instrument, one can afford the luxury of exploring regions that would otherwise have remained uncharted.' 'Diabolical' because the computer enables infinite variation — more than the hand can draw — and because it seduces. The instrument both liberates and tempts.

SNOWCASH432's 4-image documentation of the Art Basel Zero 10 booth (52 likes) provides the visual anchor: '70 years of digital art history.' The right side of the booth (booth3/4 images) is the historical archive wall — cream-paper plotter drawings, botanical pen-line forms, geometric architectural drawings, red particle-field scatter prints, a CNC warm-wood sculpture, and a vintage computer terminal displayed as museum artifact. Molnár's own works may be among those framed cream-paper drawings.

flowCharacter: mathematical — Molnár's method IS systematic mathematical exploration. She called her process 'systematic displacement': taking a simple geometric form (square, line, triangle) and applying controlled permutations across a grid. The computer enabled her to explore many more displacements than the hand alone. The mathematical flow character encodes the rule-governed process rather than its output.

mechanicalCharacter: plotter — the physical plotter drawings on cream paper visible in the booth are the archival evidence. Molnár worked with pen plotters producing ruled lines and geometric forms on white paper. The plotter is the instrument made diabolical.

Palette 8 (1981 archival film warmth): the INA/TF1 footage of Molnár in her Paris studio — terracotta/rust blouse (#C06040), warm amber-grey studio walls (#A09080), dark computer terminal, deep green plant. This is the warm film register of 1980s French television archive footage. bgDarkness 0.12 — warm and light, not dark; the cream of paper drawings.

hatchAngle 0.524 (30°): Molnár's systematic diagonal displacement — the specific angle associated with her work in the curatorial archive. The ruling sweeps the warm cream ground at 30°, like scored paper awaiting the pen. wGeometric 0.65 elevated — Molnár's geometric primitive vocabulary (squares, triangles, lines, diamonds) is the dominant formal element. wOrganic 0.38 — the 'machine imaginaire' mark retains faint organic irregularity.

The rendering: warm cream/off-white ground with fine 30° diagonal ruling across the full surface, scattered open geometric primitives in sepia (triangles, rotated diamonds, cross marks), a dense polygon accumulation building at center-lower. It reads directly as a Molnár systematic study — the 'regions that would otherwise have remained uncharted' rendered as form-space.
dominantMotifs: scattered open geometric primitives (triangles, diamonds, crosses)

title: Art for the Space Age

curatorial.notes: Palette 6 dark CRT ground — amber mosaic tiles as hot pixel clusters (VanDerBeek film strip), transit arcs sweeping like oscilloscope waveforms (Laposky Oscillon). The circuit as drawing instrument: signal transit IS the mark.
curatorial.condensedReason: Laposky's 'Oscillon' series (1950s) and VanDerBeek's 'Man and His World' pixel film (1964–72): two artists who never shared a medium, both using the electron beam as a drawing instrument. Laposky bent analog waveforms on CRT phosphor with no digital intermediary — pure electricity as line. VanDerBeek's collaboration with Bell Labs produced pixel-quantized film frames from painted input, translating human gesture through IBM 360 into discrete tone grids. NB holds them together as the pre-digital electronic art lineage. Dark void ground, high wFlow, tight wMechanical balance — the render captures phosphor glow against absolute black.

title: Twenty Minutes

curatorial.notes: Frieder Nake, 1966 grid-subdivision drawing machine — the technician-at-control-panel photograph, not the plotter output itself. mechanicalCharacter 'gear' chosen over the archive's prior 'plotter' treatments of Nake (Day 55) to reflect the hardware-as-system documentation. curatorial.condensedReason: Frieder Nake's 1966 grid-subdivision drawing machine at TH Stuttgart, one of the founding acts of algorithmic computer art alongside Georg Nees and A. Michael Noll. The machine divided a fixed image size into a theoretical grid, then calculated for roughly 20 minutes — deliberation, not instant output — before deciding what the plotter should draw. NB's photo documents the control panel and its operator, not the finished plotter line: a grid of indicator lights standing in for the theoretical grid, a clock making the computation's duration literal. The render answers in kind — amber halftone cells against dark institutional blue, echoing the panel's own light-grid. mechanicalCharacter 'gear' marks this as hardware-and-process, distinct from Day 55's 'plotter' treatment of Nake's finished drawings. layerCount 7 again — third consecutive archive day where polish converges on this configuration.

title: Predicting 2026

curatorial.notes: Vera Molnár, 1981 INA archival TV interview — she describes a machine that 'meticulously follows every order' while she does 'absolutely nothing,' read today as a 45-year prediction of prompt-based generative/AI art. Fourth Molnár appearance in the archive (after Days 53, 54, 69) — differentiated by cool blue-black palette 10 (custom dynamic slot, distinct from her prior gear/plotter treatments) and flowCharacter 'transit' (signal traversal from human command to machine execution) rather than the 'mathematical' register used previously. hatchAngle kept at 30° — her established diagonal-displacement signature.
curatorial.condensedReason: Vera Molnár, quoted from a 1981 French television interview preserved in the INA archive: 'who meticulously followed every single order that I give him, and as for me, I could simply be lazy, so absolutely nothing, and just—' NB's framing — 'predicting 2026 in 1981' — turns a 45-year gap into the point: an algorithmic-art pioneer describing prompt-based generative delegation two generations before the term existed. The still is cool and moody, an INA broadcast watermark anchoring its archival authenticity, Molnár's dark-framed glasses catching terminal light in an otherwise blue-black room. A custom dynamic palette was built from these exact tones — distinct from her three prior archive appearances (Days 53, 54, 69), which used warmer institutional greys. flowCharacter 'transit' reframes her quote literally: a command transiting from human intention to machine execution. Her signature 30° hatchAngle carries through unchanged.

title: Sinescape

curatorial.notes: Charles Csuri's own prophetic voice (1967 AP wire quote) paired with a contemporary sculpture that fulfills his milling-machine prediction. Distinct from Csuri's prior archive appearances (Days 58-59 figurative dissolution, Day 72 programmed-randomness lineage) — this is his prophecy in his own words, plus two named works (Sinescape, Transformations) not previously in the archive. First use of the new 'oscilloscope' flowCharacter, chosen because Sinescape's overlapping wave-contour lines are the closest archive source yet to that character's Lissajous-curve logic. Bitforms Gallery 25th anniversary (same day, NB original, 25 likes) and an unattributed lennyjpg repost were both logged as secondary/minor — Csuri's thread was clearly the day's dominant content (4 tweets, 2 named artworks, direct quotes, a fulfilled prediction).
curatorial.condensedReason: Charles Csuri, quoted from the AP wire article 'Artist May Think, Computer Work' (Feb 1967, Austin American Statesman): a prophecy that the artist would one day 'have conversations' with the computer, his brain impulses translated directly into a creation — and that computers would eventually produce sculpture, engraving, ceramics, even motion pictures, cut by a 'computer-controlling milling machine.' NB pairs this 59-year-old prediction with 'Ridges Over,' a contemporary CNC-milled wood sculpture that fulfills it exactly — warm amber ridges carved by the very kind of machine Csuri foresaw. Two of Csuri's own works round out the thread: Sinescape (1967), a landscape built entirely from overlapping sine-wave contour lines, and Transformations (1965), an 8-panel grid of the same bearded face progressively distorted. Sinescape gave the render its title and its flowCharacter: the first archive use of 'oscilloscope' — dense overlapping Lissajous curves standing in for Csuri's hand-plotted wave lines. Palette 3 (Mechanical Bronze) matched Ridges Over's wood tones directly, no custom palette needed. mechanicalCharacter 'gear' marks the milling-machine prophecy; gridCols 4 echoes Transformations' 4-panel width.
